title: Unchecked ERC20 transfer return values allow phantom deposits and silent failed withdrawals
claim: `deposit`, `withdraw`, and `emergencyWithdraw` call `transferFrom`/`transfer` directly and ignore the returned boolean. For any accepted token that returns `false` instead of reverting, the contract still updates `balances`, checkpoints, pool sizes, and stable-coin accounting as if tokens moved successfully.
impact: An attacker can create or use a token whose `transferFrom` silently fails for them, obtain stake credit without funding the pool, and later withdraw real liquidity deposited by other users of the same token. Failed outbound transfers can also leave users debited without actually receiving tokens.
paths: Use a non-stable token that returns `false` on `transferFrom` for the attacker but still allows normal transfers for other users.
Call `deposit(token, amount, referrer)`; the contract records `amount` as staked even though it received nothing.
After honest users deposit the same token, call `withdraw` to receive real tokens from the contract while your original deposit was never funded.

title: Deposits credit the requested amount instead of the tokens actually received
claim: `deposit` increases `stableCoinBalances`, user `balances`, checkpoints, and epoch pool accounting by the caller-supplied `amount` without measuring how many tokens the contract actually received. Fee-on-transfer, deflationary, rebasing, or hook-based tokens therefore create over-credited positions.
impact: A user can deposit a taxed or deflationary token, receive credit for more tokens than arrived, and later over-withdraw from that token pool once enough honest liquidity exists. The mismatch also corrupts epoch snapshots and reward-weight calculations.
paths: Use a non-stable token that transfers only `amount - fee` to the staking contract.
Call `deposit(token, amount, referrer)`; internal accounting and checkpoints increase by `amount`, not by the net tokens received.
Later withdraw the full recorded balance, consuming liquidity supplied by later depositors.

title: Emergency withdrawal lets users recover principal while retaining stale epoch stake, and untouched pools are immediately eligible
claim: `emergencyWithdraw` only zeroes `balances[msg.sender][token]` and transfers tokens out; it does not update `balanceCheckpoints`, initialized `poolSize` snapshots, or any other epoch state consumed by `getEpochUserBalance` and `getEpochPoolSize`. Worse, `lastWithdrawEpochId[token]` defaults to `0`, and because `epoch1Start` is hard-coded to June 21, 2021, the current epoch is already far greater than 10, so any non-stable token pool with no prior successful normal withdrawal is immediately eligible for emergency exit.
impact: A user can deposit into a fresh or untouched non-stable pool, immediately recover their principal through `emergencyWithdraw`, and still appear staked in epoch-based snapshot logic. Any rewards, voting weight, or accounting that relies on these getters can be stolen or distorted while the user no longer has funds at risk.
paths: Use a non-stable token pool that has never had a successful normal `withdraw` recorded.
Call `deposit(token, amount, referrer)` to create checkpoints and initialize epoch snapshots.
Immediately call `emergencyWithdraw(token)`; the tokens are returned, but `getEpochUserBalance` and initialized pool snapshots still reflect the old stake.

title: Any small withdrawal can indefinitely grief the emergency-exit timer for an entire token pool
claim: The emergency-exit gate is tracked in the single global variable `lastWithdrawEpochId[token]`. Every successful normal withdrawal, no matter which user performed it or how small the amount was, resets the timer for all users in that token pool.
impact: An attacker with a dust position can keep `emergencyWithdraw` unavailable indefinitely by periodically withdrawing tiny amounts. If the pool later becomes dysfunctional or ordinary withdrawals stop working, all other stakers can be trapped without access to the advertised rescue path.
paths: Acquire a minimal stake in a non-stable token pool.
Call `withdraw(token, dust)` at least once every 9 epochs.
All other users fail `emergencyWithdraw` because `getCurrentEpoch() - lastWithdrawEpochId[token]` never reaches 10.

title: Dormant pools become unusable until every missed epoch is initialized one transaction at a time
claim: When a pool is inactive for more than one epoch beyond the auto-initialized `current + 1` snapshot, both `deposit` and `withdraw` try to initialize only the current epoch. `manualEpochInit` then reverts because it requires `epochId - 1` to already be initialized, so skipped epochs must be backfilled strictly one by one.
impact: After long inactivity, normal deposits and withdrawals revert until someone submits a separate `manualEpochInit` transaction for every missed epoch. This creates a practical denial of service and can leave a dormant pool unusable until an arbitrarily large catch-up sequence is executed.
paths: Let a token pool sit idle for many epochs after its last activity.
Attempt `deposit` or `withdraw` in the current epoch; the internal `manualEpochInit(currentEpoch)` call reverts because `currentEpoch - 1` was never initialized.
Users must send one catch-up initialization transaction per missing epoch before normal operations resume.

title: Ignored Compound error codes can desynchronize stablecoin accounting from real liquidity
claim: Compound's `mint`, `redeemUnderlying`, and `redeem` return non-zero error codes on failure instead of reverting, but the contract ignores those return values everywhere. The staking logic therefore assumes stablecoins moved into or out of Compound even when Compound explicitly reported failure.
impact: Paused markets, insufficient cash, or other Compound-side failures can leave `stableCoinBalances`, on-hand token balances, and cToken positions out of sync. Withdrawals can proceed after local balances are debited even though liquidity was never redeemed, interest extraction can malfunction, and later users can face failed or unfair withdrawals.
paths: A stablecoin withdrawal calls `_redeemFromCompound(amount)`, but Compound returns a non-zero error code because redemption cannot be satisfied.
The function continues as if redemption succeeded and attempts to transfer tokens to the user anyway.
Accounting has already been reduced locally, so the contract can enter an inconsistent state and subsequent withdrawals or interest operations can fail.

title: Anyone can reinitialize epoch 0 to zero and corrupt pre-launch stake snapshots
claim: `manualEpochInit` skips the normal `!epochIsInitialized(...)` guard when `epochId == 0`, so any caller can overwrite `poolSize[token][0]` with zero even after epoch-0 deposits already populated it. Later initialization of epoch 1 copies the forged zero value forward. This only matters if the contract was deployed and funded before the hard-coded epoch-1 start time of June 21, 2021, when epoch 0 was intended to hold the initial stake.
impact: If users staked during epoch 0, an attacker can erase the base pool snapshot that later epochs inherit. Those users can be excluded from reward accounting, and subsequent withdrawals can revert on pool-size underflow because the copied epoch pool size is zero while user checkpoints remain non-zero.
paths: Users deposit into a pool before June 21, 2021, creating non-zero `poolSize[token][0]`.
An attacker calls `manualEpochInit([token], 0)`, which resets `poolSize[token][0]` to zero.
When epoch 1 is initialized, `poolSize[token][1]` is copied from the forged zero snapshot.
An epoch-0 staker later withdraws and hits `poolSize[token][currentEpoch].size.sub(amount)`, reverting until enough new deposits refill the snapshot.

title: Direct token transfers or rebases permanently poison non-stable pool-size accounting
claim: For non-stable tokens, `deposit()` and `withdraw()` snapshot the next epoch's pool size from `IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this))` instead of from the sum of tracked user balances. Unsolicited token transfers, token donations, or positive rebases therefore increase epoch denominators without creating any matching `balances` entry or user checkpoint.
impact: Any third party can distort reward-weight or voting denominators for a non-stable pool by transferring tokens directly to the staking contract. The excess tokens are not attributable to any staker, so rewards can be diluted and a portion of the pool balance can become permanently stranded from users' tracked principal.
paths: An attacker or mistaken sender transfers a non-stable token directly to the staking contract, or the token positively rebases while held there.
A later `deposit()` or `withdraw()` stores the raw on-chain balance as `poolSize[token][currentEpoch + 1].size`.
Integrations that rely on `getEpochPoolSize()` use an inflated denominator even though no user gained matching stake, diluting future rewards and leaving the donated excess outside user accounting.

title: Uninitialized historical epochs read mutable current balances instead of fixed snapshots
claim: When a requested epoch was never initialized, `getEpochPoolSize()` returns the contract's current live balance (`stableCoinBalances` for stablecoins and `IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this))` for non-stables) rather than the pool size that actually existed during the requested epoch. Historical denominators therefore remain mutable until every skipped epoch is backfilled.
impact: If rewards or other accounting query a past uninitialized epoch before manual backfilling completes, present-day balance changes can rewrite that epoch's apparent pool size. This makes historical accounting non-deterministic and can skew claims or allocations for dormant periods.
paths: A pool goes dormant and one or more past epochs remain uninitialized.
Before accounting for one of those epochs is finalized, an attacker changes the current pool balance via deposit, withdrawal, token donation, or rebase.
`getEpochPoolSize(token, oldEpoch)` returns the manipulated current balance instead of the old epoch's true denominator.

title: Permissionless interest-skimming sweeps accidental stablecoin or cToken transfers to the team wallet
claim: `getInterestFromCompound` and `getInterest` treat any value above `stableCoinBalances[token]` as team-owned interest and allow any caller to send it to the hard-coded `TEAM_ADDRESS`. The code never distinguishes genuine Compound yield from assets that arrived via direct transfers of USDC/USDT/DAI or cUSDC/cUSDT/cDAI into the staking contract.
impact: Mistakenly sent stablecoins, donated cTokens, or other untracked assets can be irreversibly redirected to the team wallet by any external caller. The original sender has no on-chain recovery path once the sweep transaction executes.
paths: A user accidentally transfers DAI directly to the staking contract, or transfers cDAI/cUSDC/cUSDT to it.
Any account calls `getInterest(dai)` or `getInterestFromCompound(dai)`.
The function computes the excess over `stableCoinBalances[dai]` as `interest` and transfers that amount to `TEAM_ADDRESS`.

title: A failed Compound mint can leave a non-zero allowance that blocks all future stablecoin deposits
claim: `_transferToCompound` uses `safeApprove(cToken, amount)` before calling `CTokenInterface(cToken).mint(amount)`, but it never clears the allowance and it ignores `mint`'s error code. If Compound returns a non-zero error without consuming the allowance, the staking contract is left with a non-zero approval to the cToken. Later deposits for that stablecoin revert because OpenZeppelin `safeApprove` forbids changing a non-zero allowance directly to another non-zero allowance.
impact: A single failed Compound mint can brick the affected stablecoin pool for new deposits. Existing accounting remains live, but no one can add more principal through the normal deposit path unless the contract is upgraded or migrated.
paths: A stablecoin deposit reaches `_transferToCompound(token, amount)`.
`safeApprove(cToken, amount)` succeeds, but `cToken.mint(amount)` returns a non-zero error code and leaves the allowance untouched.
The staking contract now has a non-zero token allowance for that cToken.
The next stablecoin deposit hits `safeApprove(cToken, newAmount)` and reverts with `approve from non-zero to non-zero allowance`, blocking future deposits into that pool.
